subject: Careers
topic: Career 8 Mosaic - Week 3 Community and Meaning

secondary_architecture_support.note: The week can be compressed into shorter advisory-style blocks, but the staged packet, Day 4 checkpoint, and Day 5 final synthesis still need the full sequence.

materials_control_note: The weekly packet holds community mapping, role-to-need matching, case-study planning, and personal reflection notes only. The Day 4 checkpoint sheet stays teacher-facing. The single primary evidence artifact for the week stays in the Day 5 final response sheet.
grade_subject_fit: Fits BC Career Education 8 by centering community needs, the value of work, local and social conditions that shape jobs, and reflection on how interests and strengths connect to meaningful contribution.
unit_context: Week 3 of a six-week Career 8 Mosaic unit. This week builds on Week 1 self-knowledge and Week 2 decision-making by helping students see that work exists because needs exist, and that both visible and less-visible roles keep communities functioning.
assignment_purpose: Students investigate how communities function through a staged week of inquiry about needs, systems, contribution, volunteering, and meaningful work, then create a Community Needs Map or Jobs That Keep Us Going final artifact that connects a real need to roles, systems, and personal meaning.
final_evidence_target: A Day 5 Community Needs Map or Jobs That Keep Us Going artifact in the final response sheet that identifies at least one real community need, names jobs or roles connected to it, explains how work contributes to community well-being, and reflects on what kind of contribution feels meaningful to the student.

student_task_flow.step: Notice what a community needs in order to keep going
student_task_flow.label: Day 1
student_task_flow.evidence: Students map everyday needs and begin noticing the roles and systems connected to them.

student_task_flow.step: Match visible and less-visible roles to local needs
student_task_flow.label: Day 2
student_task_flow.evidence: Students compare roles that get noticed quickly with roles that are often only noticed when they disappear.

student_task_flow.step: Examine contribution through case work and community problem-solving
student_task_flow.label: Day 3
student_task_flow.evidence: Students analyze how paid roles, unpaid roles, volunteering, and systems work together without reducing community to charity alone.

student_task_flow.step: Connect strengths and interests to meaningful contribution
student_task_flow.label: Day 4
student_task_flow.evidence: Students prepare a specific need-role-contribution explanation and conference for release.

student_task_flow.label: Day 5
student_task_flow.evidence: Students complete the Community Needs Map or Jobs That Keep Us Going artifact in the final response sheet only.

success_criteria: I can identify at least one real community need and explain why it matters.
I can name jobs or roles connected to that need, including roles people may overlook.
I can explain how work contributes to people, systems, and community well-being.
I can connect my own interests or strengths to a kind of contribution that feels meaningful.
I can complete a clear Day 5 final artifact on the final response sheet.

supports_scaffolds.label: Community mapping templates
supports_scaffolds.support: Students begin with concrete need-role-system organizers rather than an open reflection page.

supports_scaffolds.label: Role-to-need matching
supports_scaffolds.support: Packet tasks and slide prompts keep the focus on how jobs and roles exist because needs exist.

supports_scaffolds.label: Sentence frames
supports_scaffolds.support: Frames help students move from broad statements about helping to clearer need-role-contribution explanations.

supports_scaffolds.label: Scenario cards
supports_scaffolds.support: Case work makes community contribution practical rather than abstract or sentimental.

supports_scaffolds.label: Local example prompts
supports_scaffolds.support: Students can anchor their thinking in school, neighborhood, transit, recreation, food, health, housing, and public spaces.

supports_scaffolds.label: Partner discussion before writing
supports_scaffolds.support: Each day includes short talk prompts before longer writing so students can test an example aloud first.

supports_scaffolds.label: Roles-needs-contribution organizer
supports_scaffolds.support: Students build a map they can later transfer into the final Day 5 artifact.

supports_scaffolds.label: Choice in response format
supports_scaffolds.support: Students may plan in bullets in the packet and choose between a Community Needs Map or Jobs That Keep Us Going structure for the final response sheet.

differentiation_model.support_pathway: Use local example prompts, role-to-need matching, sentence frames, and partner talk to help students move from broad community language to one concrete need-role explanation.
differentiation_model.core_pathway: Move through the weekly packet, compare visible and less-visible work, and complete one clear final artifact showing how work contributes to community well-being.
differentiation_model.extension_pathway: Push students to compare multiple systems around the same need, notice how local conditions shape roles, and explain why different kinds of contribution carry different meaning for different people.

checkpoint_release_logic: Keep the weekly packet as planning-only evidence until Day 4.

Release the Day 5 final response sheet only after the teacher checkpoint confirms the student can name one concrete community need, connect at least one job or role to that need, and explain how that work contributes to community well-being.
teacher_implementation_notes: Keep the week practical, discussion-rich, and grounded in real systems rather than abstract moral language.
Do not reduce community to charity alone; include everyday roles and infrastructure that keep school and local life functioning.
Treat the packet as one staged workflow across the week, not as five disconnected worksheets.
Keep the career connection visible: work exists because needs exist, and local conditions shape what roles matter.
Use local examples when possible, but let students use nearby school or community systems if broader local knowledge is uneven.
likely_misconceptions: Students may think community contribution only means volunteering or charity and miss everyday work that keeps systems running.
Students may think only highly visible or high-status jobs matter to a community.
Students may think meaningful work has to look inspiring all the time instead of being connected to real needs and responsibilities.
Students may think unpaid help can replace all paid systems and public services.
Students may think their own strengths and interests have no connection to contribution yet because they are still in school.
assessment_focus: Quality of the student's explanation of at least one real community need
Accuracy and clarity in naming jobs or roles connected to that need
Strength of the student's explanation of how work contributes to people or systems
Clarity of the student's short reflection on what kind of contribution feels meaningful

lesson_overview.overview: Week 3 of the six-week Career 8 Mosaic unit focuses on community needs, the value of work, and the idea that jobs and roles exist because needs exist. Across five days, students map community systems, compare visible and less-visible work, analyze contribution through case studies, and connect their own strengths and interests to meaningful contribution. The week ends with a Community Needs Map or Jobs That Keep Us Going artifact that explains one or more real needs, names jobs or roles connected to those needs, and reflects on what kind of contribution feels meaningful to the student.
lesson_overview.essential_question: How do community needs shape work, and how can our interests and strengths connect to meaningful contribution?

sequence.focus: Community needs and what keeps a community going.
sequence.carryover: Students begin noticing that work is connected to needs, systems, and everyday functioning.

sequence.focus: Local needs, visible and invisible work, and supporting roles.
sequence.carryover: Students compare the work people notice immediately with the work that only becomes visible when it stops.

sequence.focus: Contribution, volunteering, and problem-solving case work.
sequence.carryover: Students see volunteering as one real contribution pathway without letting it stand in for all community work.

sequence.focus: Connecting strengths and interests to community contribution and meaningful work.
sequence.carryover: Students prepare one specific need-role-contribution explanation before the final artifact.

sequence.focus: Community Needs Map or Jobs That Keep Us Going completion and insight share.
sequence.carryover: The final evidence remains in the Day 5 final response sheet only.

integrity_checks: The weekly packet remains one coherent staged workflow instead of five disconnected pages.
The Day 4 checkpoint sheet stays teacher-facing and supports release logic rather than student busywork.
The Day 5 final response sheet is the single primary evidence artifact for the week.
The community lens stays practical and connected to systems, roles, and everyday work.
The career connection stays explicit without forcing students into one future identity.

teacher_guide.big_idea: Work matters because it helps meet needs in families, communities, and society. In Week 3, students examine how community needs shape jobs, roles, systems, and possibilities, while also reflecting on what kinds of contribution feel meaningful to them.
learning_goals: I can help students see that community includes everyday systems and invisible work, not only charity or obvious helping roles.
I can keep the week's discussions practical, local, and grounded in real examples instead of vague inspiration language.
I can help students connect needs, roles, and contribution without losing the Career Education focus.
I can use the Day 4 checkpoint to confirm whether students have enough concrete material to build a strong Day 5 final artifact.
materials: One weekly Career Mosaic packet for Week 3
Optional WorkBC or Career Trek tools later in the week after the core reflection work is established

timing.activity: Launch the week by asking what a community needs in order to keep going and widen the idea of community beyond charity.
Use quick mapping and talk prompts to surface food, safety, transportation, health, recreation, learning, and belonging as real community needs.
Students complete the Day 1 packet organizer connecting one need to places, roles, and what happens when the need is not met.
Launch visible versus invisible work analysis with examples from school and community life.
Run role-to-need matching, what-would-happen-if-this-job-disappeared prompts, or short partner sorts.
Students record one local need and map multiple roles connected to it, including at least one role people often overlook.
Introduce community case work that includes service, volunteering, and system roles without treating volunteering as the whole answer.
Use scenario cards or local problem/local solution tasks to compare direct responders, behind-the-scenes roles, and short-term volunteer support.
Students build a contribution map showing one need, who responds, and how different roles help.
Model how strengths and interests can connect to meaningful contribution without asking students to lock in one future identity.
Students complete the strengths-to-contribution planner and choose the final artifact structure they will use on Day 5.
Teacher conferences with the checkpoint sheet to confirm who can move from broad talk about community into a specific need-role-contribution explanation.
Re-open the final artifact ingredients, model the tone of a clear synthesis, and remind students that the Day 5 final response sheet is the only final evidence location.
Students complete the Community Needs Map or Jobs That Keep Us Going artifact using notes from the packet.
Students share one insight about community need, hidden work, or the kind of contribution that feels meaningful to them.

teacher_notes: Keep the language practical and community-focused. Avoid drifting into generic moral lessons about being a good person.
Use school examples if students struggle to name wider local systems. Cafeteria work, custodial work, office work, transit, recreation, and health services all count.
Do not let volunteering replace the main message. It is one meaningful form of contribution, but communities also rely on ongoing roles, systems, and skilled work.
Treat the packet as a week-long build. Do not let Day 5 turn into a cold start.
likely_misconceptions: Students may think only emergency or dramatic work really helps a community.
Students may think visible work matters more than less-visible maintenance, support, or systems work.
Students may think meaningful work has to match a passion perfectly instead of meeting a real need.
Students may think contribution only counts if it is unpaid or self-sacrificing.
likely_hesitations: Some students may default to jobs they already know well and miss the wider system around those jobs.
Some students may talk vaguely about helping unless pushed toward one concrete need and one specific role.
Some students may find it easier to talk about community needs than to connect their own strengths or interests to contribution.
teacher_moves: Use prompts like What need is being met here? and What would get harder if this role disappeared? to keep the conversation concrete.
Model one example that includes both a visible role and a less-visible role so students do not flatten the system to one person doing everything.
Keep the career connection alive with examples about local conditions, school systems, community roles, public services, and everyday work that keeps people safe, fed, connected, and supported.
model_reflection_language: A community need I notice is reliable transportation because people cannot reach school, work, appointments, or activities without it.
The value of work is not only in the job title. It is also in what the work helps other people do and what systems it keeps moving.
A role can matter even if most people only notice it when something goes wrong.
A kind of contribution that feels meaningful to me is helping people solve real problems in a way that is steady and useful, not only dramatic.
look_fors: Student can identify one real community need in concrete language.
Student can name at least one job or role connected to that need and explain what it does.
Student can recognize that communities rely on both visible and less-visible work.
Student is building enough material to write a coherent Day 5 final artifact.
support_moves: Ask: What need is this role responding to?
Ask: Who else helps this system work, even if people do not notice them first?
Ask: Is this an example of volunteering, a job, a public service, or several roles working together?
Offer sentence frames and allow planning in bullets before students write the final Day 5 synthesis.
future_pathway_connection: If students are ready, use Career Trek or WorkBC tools later in the week to trace one community need into broader career clusters or pathways.
Keep the extension optional. The core outcome for this week is understanding need-role-contribution logic, not tool exploration.

task_sheet.title: Week 3 Career Mosaic Packet - Community and Meaning
task_sheet.purpose_line: Use this packet across the week to map needs, roles, and contribution before building your Day 5 Community Needs Map or Jobs That Keep Us Going artifact.
instructions: Move through the packet across the week. Each section builds the next one.
Use partner talk before longer writing, then keep your own notes on this sheet.
You may use short phrases or bullets in the packet. Save the polished final artifact for the Day 5 final response sheet. It is the only final evidence sheet for Week 3.

tasks.label: Day 1 / Part A
tasks.prompt: After the launch discussion, map one community need people rely on every day.

render_hints.heading: Day 1 - Community need quick map
render_hints.help: Start with something real and ordinary. School, food, transportation, health, recreation, housing, belonging, and safety all count.
record_fields: A real community need people rely on
A place where I can see this need in school or local life
What gets harder when this need is not met
One job or role already connected to this need

tasks.label: Day 1 / Part B
tasks.prompt: Complete the reflection frames about what keeps a community going.

render_hints.heading: Day 1 - What keeps a community going?
render_hints.help: Keep the idea concrete. Try not to stay at the level of a slogan.
blank_prompts: Work matters in a community because ___.
One role people may overlook until it disappears is ___ because ___.

tasks.label: Day 2 / Part A
tasks.prompt: Use one example from class to compare visible work with less-visible work.

render_hints.heading: Day 2 - Visible and invisible work
render_hints.help: The goal is not to rank roles. The goal is to notice how different kinds of work keep the same system going.
record_fields: A visible job or role people notice quickly
A less-visible role that still keeps things working
What each role helps people do
Which role gets less credit than it deserves

tasks.label: Day 2 / Part B
tasks.prompt: Choose one local or school need and map more than one role connected to it.

render_hints.heading: Day 2 - Role-to-need matching
render_hints.help: Use a local example prompt if you need one. Think about what keeps school, recreation, food, transit, health, or public spaces working.
record_fields: The local or school need I am focusing on
Two jobs or roles connected to this need
What one of those roles does behind the scenes
What would get harder if nobody did this work

tasks.label: Day 3 / Part A
tasks.prompt: Use the case-study frames to compare a community problem with roles that respond to it.

render_hints.heading: Day 3 - Contribution case study
render_hints.help: Keep volunteering in the picture, but do not let it replace the full system around the need.
blank_prompts: A community need or problem in today's case was ___.
A paid or ongoing role connected to it is ___.
A volunteer or short-term contribution that could also help is ___.
This shows that communities rely on ___, not only one kind of help.

tasks.label: Day 3 / Part B
tasks.prompt: Build a contribution map that shows how several roles connect to one need.

render_hints.heading: Day 3 - Need, roles, and contribution map
render_hints.help: Include direct responders and support roles if you can.
record_fields: One need I want to understand better
Roles that respond directly to that need
Roles that support the system around it
How volunteering or service could help without replacing the whole system
What this shows about the value of work

tasks.label: Day 4 / Part A
tasks.prompt: Connect your own strengths and interests to a kind of contribution that feels meaningful.

render_hints.heading: Day 4 - Strengths to contribution
render_hints.help: You are not choosing one future identity. You are noticing what kinds of problems, roles, or settings make your effort feel useful.
record_fields: A strength I could bring to a community need
An interest or topic that keeps my attention
A kind of contribution that feels meaningful to me
A local issue, setting, or system where I could imagine using that strength or interest

tasks.label: Day 4 / Part B
tasks.prompt: Plan the final artifact by choosing one need and a clear need-role-contribution explanation.

render_hints.heading: Day 4 - Final artifact planner
render_hints.help: Choose the clearest example you can explain. Save the polished final version for the Day 5 final response sheet.
record_fields: The community need I will focus on
Jobs or roles I will include
How those roles help the community keep going
What kind of contribution feels meaningful to me
My final format: Community Needs Map or Jobs That Keep Us Going

tasks.label: Day 4 / Part C
tasks.prompt: Complete the short frames so you are ready for the checkpoint and the Day 5 final artifact.

render_hints.heading: Day 4 - Ready for Day 5
render_hints.help: This is planning only. Move the polished final artifact to the Day 5 final response sheet.
blank_prompts: One role connected to that need is ___.
A kind of contribution that feels meaningful to me is ___.

days.carryover_note: Students begin noticing that communities rely on real needs, ongoing systems, and many kinds of work that keep things going.

content.subtitle: Community and Meaning: what communities need and what keeps them going.

content.scenario: If clean water, transit, food access, health services, and maintenance disappeared for a week, what would break first?
prompts: Which ones are easy to forget?
What work is hiding in your answer?

rows: Communities rely on food, safety, learning, health, movement, belonging, and care.
Some work is highly visible. Some work is only noticed when it stops.
The value of work is partly in what it helps other people or systems keep doing.

content.task: Map one real community need and one role tied to it.

prompts: How did today change your idea of valuable work?

days.carryover_note: Students compare visible and invisible work and begin seeing that one need often depends on several connected roles.

content.subtitle: Local needs and less-visible work: who keeps things working when nobody is clapping?

content.scenario: Pick one school or local role. What would go wrong if it disappeared for a week?

slides.title: Visible and invisible work both matter

items: Some roles are public and easy to spot.
Some are behind the scenes but still essential.
Credit is not the same thing as value.

content.task: Match roles to one need. Record one visible role and one less-visible role.

prompts: How does this change what meaningful work looks like?

days.carryover_note: Students compare community problem-solving, volunteering, and system roles without flattening all contribution into charity.

content.subtitle: Contribution and problem-solving: volunteering, service, and the systems around them.

content.scenario: When a community problem shows up, different roles respond in different ways.

rows: Some contribution happens through paid jobs and ongoing public or community systems.
Some contribution happens through volunteering, service, mentoring, or short-term support.
Meaningful work is not only about status or income. It is also about what it helps people and communities do.

content.task: Map one need, direct roles, support roles, and where service could fit.
prompts: What need are you focusing on?
Why is volunteering not the whole answer?
What did today show about less-visible work?

days.carryover_note: Students connect strengths and interests to contribution, then prepare one clear need-role-contribution explanation for the checkpoint and Day 5 artifact.

content.subtitle: Meaningful contribution: connecting strengths, interests, and community needs.

content.scenario: People can care about community in very different ways, and none of them are automatically better.
prompts: What kind of contribution feels meaningful?
What need could it connect to?

rows: A role can matter because it meets a real need, even if it is not glamorous.
Meaningful work can look practical, steady, creative, relational, technical, or behind the scenes.
The question is not only What job sounds impressive? It is also What need do I care about helping with, and how?

content.model: One need I notice is safe public spaces. Roles tied to it include recreation staff, planners, maintenance crews, and community organizers. The work matters because those spaces support belonging. Meaningful contribution for me is helping people feel included there.
supports: Connect the need, roles, and your part.

prompts: Which need will you focus on?
What contribution feels meaningful right now?

checkpoint_sheet.title: Day 4 Checkpoint Sheet
checkpoint_sheet.checkpoint_focus: Teacher checkpoint before Day 5 Community Needs Map or Jobs That Keep Us Going release.
look_fors: Student can identify one real community need in concrete language.
Student can connect at least one job or role to that need and explain what it does.
Student can show that community includes both visible and less-visible work or systems.
Student is ready to move from packet planning to a cleaner Day 5 final artifact.
conference_prompts: Which community need are you focusing on, and why does it matter?
What jobs or roles will you include in your final artifact?
What would get harder if this work disappeared?
What kind of contribution feels meaningful to you, and how will you say that clearly on Day 5?
checkpoint_sheet.release_rule: Release the Day 5 final response sheet only when the student can explain one concrete need, name at least one connected job or role, and describe how that work contributes to community well-being.

days.carryover_note: Final evidence stays in the Day 5 Community Needs Map or Jobs That Keep Us Going artifact only.

content.subtitle: Community Needs Map or Jobs That Keep Us Going: show the need, the roles, and the meaning.

items: At least one real community need
Jobs or roles connected to that need
How those roles help people or systems keep going
What this shows about the value of work
A short reflection on what kind of contribution feels meaningful to you

content.model: Community need: reliable food access. Roles: grocery workers, drivers, food program staff, cooks, and volunteers. Contribution: they help people get what they need each day. For me, meaningful work solves practical problems and makes daily life more stable.

content.goal: Turn your packet notes into one clear final artifact.
prompts: Use clear labels, bullets, or a map-style structure if that helps.
Make the personal meaning reflection short and specific.
content.instruction: Use the Day 5 final response sheet. It is the only final evidence sheet for Week 3.

prompts: What work do you respect more now?
What contribution feels meaningful to you?

final_response_sheet.title: Day 5 Community Needs Map or Jobs That Keep Us Going
final_response_sheet.prompt: Complete your final artifact in clear labeled lines or bullet clusters. Identify at least one real community need, name jobs or roles connected to that need, explain how the work helps people or systems keep going, and include a short reflection on what kind of contribution feels meaningful to you.
planning_reminders: Use labels such as Community need, Jobs or roles, How the work contributes, What this shows about the value of work, and Meaningful contribution for me.
Choose one need you can explain clearly instead of naming many loosely.
Include both the need and the roles connected to it so the career connection stays visible.
Write the polished final artifact here. This is the only final evidence sheet for Week 3.

frame_strip: Jobs or roles connected to it: ___
How the work helps people or systems keep going: ___
What this shows about the value of work: ___
paragraph_support.reminder_box: show the need, the roles, the contribution, and the personal meaning

success_criteria: My final artifact identifies at least one real community need.
I name jobs or roles connected to that need.
I explain how the work contributes to community well-being.
My final evidence is recorded only on this sheet.
